metadata.title: Research the EPR Controversy: Does the EPR Effect Actually Work in Humans?
metadata.color_semantics: teardown: INK #2A1A0E neutral/normal; CRIMSON #C8102E key finding/vulnerability; SLATE #545454 structure/neutral

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Nik Bear Brown. The EPR effect — the number that nearly broke a field.

lines: The EPR effect — the number that nearly broke a field.

beats.narration_text: The entire field of cancer nanomedicine was built on one biological principle: tumor vessels are leaky, so nanoparticles accumulate in tumors. The EPR effect. It is real in mice. In 2016, a meta-analysis of 117 studies found that the median nanoparticle delivery to human tumors is 0.7% of the injected dose. What went wrong, and does the effect still matter?
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — title card: EPR EFFECT: 0.7% OF INJECTED DOSE ACTUALLY DELIVERED; contrast between mouse model success and human clinical reality

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The entire field of cancer nanomedicine was built on one biological principle: tumor vessels are leaky, so nanoparticles accumulate in tumors. The EPR effect. I

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to research the EPR controversy — the original concept, the meta-analysis finding, and the five reasons for the gap.

props.command:
claude "Research the EPR (enhanced permeability and retention)
effect controversy in cancer nanomedicine. Explain the
original EPR concept (Maeda, 1986), then summarize the
Wilhelm 2016 meta-analysis finding (0.7% median tumor
delivery). What are the five reasons for the EPR gap
between mouse models and human tumors (IFP, EPR
heterogeneity, mouse tumor vascularity, lack of EPR in
desmoplastic tumors, protein corona)? Despite this,
Doxil and Abraxane succeeded — explain why."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. The script builds the delivery attrition table from Claude's response — each stage from injected dose through blood circulation, tumor vasculature, interstitial space, down to cellular uptake — with the 0.7% figure at the bottom. Read every number before accepting the story.

props.code:
# epr_attrition.py — Claude Code output
STAGES = [
  ("Injected dose",      "100%"),
  ("Blood circulation",  "~60% remain"),
  ("Tumor vasculature", "~10% reach"),
  ("Interstitial space","~3% via EPR"),
  ("Cellular uptake",   "0.7% MEDIAN"),  # Wilhelm 2016
]
for stage, pct in STAGES:
    print(f"  {stage:<22} {pct}")
print("\nSource: Wilhelm et al., ACS Nano, 2016")
print("117 nanoparticle studies | IQR: 0.3-1.4%")

beats.role_note: OPEN vox slot → Manim video B04_DeliveryFunnel
beats.narration_text: Run it. The funnel shows the attrition at every stage: one hundred percent injected, sixty percent still circulating after clearance, ten percent reaching the tumor vasculature via EPR, three percent making it into the interstitial space, and at the bottom — 0.7% median delivery to the cell. The crimson box at the bottom is not a failure of one drug. It is the median across 117 studies.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_DeliveryFunnel: vertical funnel — 5 stages decreasing in width, nanoparticle attrition from injection to cellular uptake, 0.7% highlighted in CRIMSON at the bottom with Wilhelm 2016 citation

beats.narration_text: That 0.7% number did not kill the EPR field — it disciplined it. Now iterate: ask Claude whether EPR-based passive targeting is still viable in 2025, or whether the field has moved to active targeting, physical enhancement, or abandoning nanoparticles entirely.

props.command:
claude "Evaluate: is EPR-based passive targeting still a
viable design principle in 2025? Or has the field moved
to receptor-mediated active targeting, physical
enhancement (focused ultrasound), or abandoning
nanoparticles for ADCs and LNPs? What does the
Doxil/Abraxane success actually tell us about EPR?"

beats.narration_text: The honest accounting: Doxil's clinical benefit was reduced cardiotoxicity of doxorubicin — not superior tumor delivery via EPR. Abraxane's benefit came from an albumin pathway through SPARC and gp60, distinct from passive EPR. The 0.7% forced the field to ask whether nanoparticle success was actually due to EPR — and the answer changed what questions the field asks.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — two-column: DOXIL (cardiac toxicity reduction, not EPR) vs ABRAXANE (albumin-SPARC pathway, not EPR); the honest ledger of what actually drove clinical success

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The honest accounting: Doxil's clinical benefit was reduced cardiotoxicity of doxorubicin — not superior tumor delivery via EPR. Abraxane's benefit came from an

beats.narration_text: So the 0.7% number is not an indictment — it is a measurement. The EPR effect is real in mice and marginal in humans, and the clinical nanoparticles that worked succeeded for reasons other than passive EPR accumulation. That is not a failure. It is an accurate map of where the field actually was.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — summary card: EPR real in mice / marginal in humans / Doxil and Abraxane succeeded via different mechanisms; one honest line per finding

build.suggested: suggested prompt: So the 0.7% number is not an indictment — it is a measurement. The EPR effect is real in mice and marginal in humans, and the clinical nanoparticles that worked

beats.narration_text: Your move: look up the IFP values in your tumor type — the pressure gradient that blocks delivery. Then check whether the nanoparticle you are studying has active targeting ligands, and whether those ligands survive protein corona formation in serum. Those two questions are where the EPR story ends and the real delivery problem begins.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — next steps card: 1) check IFP values for your tumor type; 2) verify active targeting ligand survival vs. protein corona; pointer to IFP reel and protein corona reel
